chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly provides a working link to the source code repository (https://github.com/ibr-ds/zugchain) in Section IV. It also mentions plans to publish test drive data at https://railchain.berlin/, though this is future tense, the code availability satisfies the criterion.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation relies on a physical testbed built with real train hardware (M-COM RT V1 QW nodes, Freescale i.MX 6 CPUs, MVB bus) and includes deployment verification on an actual ICE TD train. This exceeds standard software simulations.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: Section III-B ('System Model and Fault Assumptions') contains a clear, explicit definition of the fault model. It formally specifies the Byzantine fault assumption (n >= 3f+1 nodes, up to f arbitrary faults), network synchrony assumptions (partially synchronous with delta), and specific behaviors of faulty nodes (duplication, omission, DoS).
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides a rigorous, data-driven analysis of overhead. It quantifies CPU usage (max 15% of shared resources), memory utilization compared to baseline, network latency, and export times for varying block counts. Trade-offs between filtering duplicates and ensuring completeness are also analyzed.

major_weakness: The paper does not provide a link to the dataset of test drive results from the ICE TD train deployment, only mentioning plans to publish them in the future.
